---
title: "Governance Rights: Fork, Exit, and Audit"
chapter_id: "governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "ethica_mechanica"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "alignment_field"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "spinoza"
  - "uat"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Six of seven claim-source mappings now have reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped until a durable local export or line-addressable connector capture is available."
  - "The governance_right_record fixture validates schema/example consistency only; audit availability, exit-path preservation, and fork-right safety tests remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `ethica_mechanica`, `coherence_exchange`, `alignment_field`, `ladon_manhattan`, `spinoza`, `uat`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `ethica_mechanica`, `coherence_exchange`; supporting: `alignment_field`, `ladon_manhattan`, `spinoza`, `uat`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `ethica_mechanica`, `coherence_exchange`, `alignment_field`, `ladon_manhattan`, `spinoza`, `uat`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `ethica_mechanica`, `alignment_field`, `ladon_manhattan`, `spinoza`, `uat`; passage-reviewed tracked local-project source: `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; connector-only/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | `governance_right_record.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.GovernanceRights` implements finite audit/exit preservation and protected-right removal gates; audit-record availability, exit-path preservation, and fork-right safety tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

Fork, exit, audit, and dissent are technical rights. That framing does not claim that any institution or deployed system already provides those rights; the repo currently validates record shape only.

It follows the value-conflict chapter because unresolved disagreement is not useful unless affected people can inspect the record, challenge it, leave, fork where safe, or preserve dissent without depending on the authority being challenged.

The rights are runtime promises only when they have a request path, a response artifact, and a failure mode. A right that cannot emit a receipt, denial reason, redaction boundary, or appeal path is not yet an interface.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Moral uncertainty creates obligations that governance rights keep contestable after systems grow difficult to leave. Audit, exit, fork, appeal, and dissent are not decorative ideals; they are runtime capabilities for moral residuals.

The operational test is simple: does the right still work when use is inconvenient for the system operator? If a policy update, memory migration, or capability replacement can quietly remove the path, the right was not part of the architecture. A right needs a receipt, an access path, and an appeal route, not only a sentence in policy text.

Rights become real when they survive pressure from systems they constrain. The stronger the system becomes, the more concrete those escape and review paths must be. Exit must preserve enough data, context, artifacts, and continuity that leaving is not punishment. Audit must expose records without blind trust. Fork must carry lineage rather than erasing dissent.

A right without a working route is only a hope dressed as governance. Consent becomes meaningful when loyalty can end, disagreement can be recorded, and inconvenience cannot delete the path.
:::

## Problem

Powerful AI infrastructure needs rights and mechanisms that prevent governance lock-in and hidden control.

Value conflict remains unresolved by design. Affected people and institutions need technical rights when governance itself becomes the risk. A system that can update policies, route evidence, manage memory, and execute actions should not require trust in a single opaque control point.

Ethica Mechanica frames governance as contestable recursive correction rather than fixed sovereign authority. The Coherence Exchange contributes fork, exit, audit, and contestability as epistemic-governance concepts that still need a durable line-addressable export. Ladon/Manhattan adds the security lesson: rights are weak if credential use, permission lookup, and execution compartments are invisible to the party whose authority is being exercised. The stack translates those ideas into interfaces that lower layers can preserve, deny with reasons, or escalate.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Policy statements about transparency are weak if users cannot audit, contest, exit, or fork the system in practice.

Transparency is not the same as auditability. A user can be shown an explanation while still lacking logs, source records, appeal routes, export paths, or meaningful alternatives. Exit is not real if data, tools, identity, or institutional dependency are held hostage. Fork is not safe if it bypasses source obligations, privacy constraints, or safety gates.

The rights have to be technical. They need artifacts, permissions, retention rules, redaction rules, and failure tests.

A right earns engineering status when it still works while inconvenient for the system. Audit matters most when the decision is contested. Exit matters most when the platform would prefer retention. Fork matters most when maintainers disagree. Dissent matters most when the governing process wants closure. If those rights depend on voluntary cooperation from the authority being challenged, they are not robust interfaces; they are policy aspirations.

This does not mean rights should override every safety boundary. A fork can be denied or narrowed when it would expose private data, secrets, dangerous tools, or obligations that cannot be transferred. An audit can redact protected material. An exit path can preserve legal or safety constraints. The engineering requirement is that denial itself becomes an artifact with scope, reason, authority, and appeal path, rather than disappearing into an opaque refusal.

This is why rights must be tested from the position of the weaker party. The system should be able to answer whether the holder can find the record, authenticate it, export what is portable, challenge what is contested, and see why protected material was withheld.

The rights layer also prevents governance from becoming a black box around its own exceptions. If a denial is legitimate, the record should say what was denied, why, by whom, under which safety or privacy rule, and where the holder can appeal. Opaque refusal may sometimes be necessary at the content boundary; opaque governance should not be the default architecture.

## Core Claim

[governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Fork, exit, audit, dissent, and contestability should be treated as technical governance interfaces, not only political ideals.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Ethica Mechanica supplies the strongest reviewed fork/exit/audit lineage; Alignment Field and the AI Constitution supply reviewed non-domination, consent, contestability, reversibility, and auditability constraints; Ladon/Manhattan supplies reviewed authority-boundary mechanics; and Spinoza/UAT supply reviewed governance-review discipline. Coherence Exchange remains useful connector-only synthesis without a durable local raw-cache export here. The source language becomes interface language without being presented as implemented governance.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this core governance-rights claim to every assigned source note. Six mappings now carry reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The mappings support the interface vocabulary for audit, exit, fork, dissent, authority boundaries, and contestability, not a claim that any deployed institution or runtime already provides those rights.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `ethica_mechanica` | Contestable recursive governance, public correction, agency, institutional memory, and resistance to frozen sovereign authority. | Not a tested institution, legal guarantee, or empirical governance result. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Fork, exit, audit, contestability, verification supply-chain, value/accounting, and governance-interface framing. | Speculative and connector-derived synthesis; no implemented economic or governance mechanism is claimed. |
| `alignment_field` | Dignity, agency, plurality, corrigibility, and governance constraints that rights interfaces should preserve under power. | Normative lineage only; not empirical evidence that rights are preserved. |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Hidden credential boundaries, handle-based authority, permission lookup, isolated compartments, and credential-injection points around authority use. | Architecture/specification only; no kernel implementation, side-channel validation, audit-log implementation, or security audit is claimed. |
| `spinoza` | Claim graphs, protected axioms, contradiction handling, downgrades, and blocked self-authorizing changes as review discipline for governance decisions. | Does not prove whole-system epistemic correctness, autoformalization, or governance legitimacy. |
| `uat` | Adversarial review, proposition tiering, dossier-bounded verification, unsupported-claim removal, and human sign-off checkpoints for contested governance claims. | Protocol design only; no implemented review process or benchmark result is reproduced. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Auditability, agency preservation, consent, reversibility, least sufficient power, risk-tiered tool use, red-team evaluations, version control, and self-improvement freezes. | Constitutional specification only; not proof of deployed governance rights, fork safety, or audit availability. |

## Mechanism

Governance rights become real only when they have access paths, artifacts, preservation rules, and denial semantics. Audit, exit, fork, dissent, and contestability are capabilities that the runtime must preserve across policy updates, memory changes, authority use, and capability replacement.

Each right is represented as a capability with an owner, scope, artifact requirement, safety constraint, access path, preservation rule, and test. Audit requires durable records attached to governance decisions. Exit requires export, revocation, and continuity paths. Fork requires a reproducible boundary that preserves source, privacy, and safety obligations. Dissent requires a channel that cannot be silently deleted by the authority being challenged.

SCFs later depend on this rights layer: capability replacement must preserve protected governance rights across implementation changes.

The rights record is therefore a bridge between governance and runtime state. It says which logs must survive, which memory exports are allowed, which secrets must remain sealed, which dependencies must be portable, and which decision can be contested after a model, policy, tool, or capability field changes. That bridge matters because governance failure often looks like ordinary maintenance: a migration drops old logs, an update changes appeal semantics, a new integration captures user data, or a replacement implementation removes an export hook. The stack treats those as rights-preservation failures, not as harmless refactors.

A governance right should produce a rights receipt. The receipt records the request, holder, scope, artifact set, material available, material withheld, denial or redaction reason, appeal path, expiry, and preservation obligation. That receipt is what lets later SCF replacement, rollback, tribunal review, or audit logic ask whether the right survived the transition. It is also an anti-capture device: the authority being challenged should not be the only keeper of the record that proves how the challenge was handled.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Transition["Governance transition"] --> Right["Governance right record"]
  Right --> Audit["Audit artifacts"]
  Right --> Exit["Exit / export / revocation path"]
  Right --> Fork["Fork boundary + obligations"]
  Right --> Dissent["Dissent channel"]
  Right --> Redaction["Denial or redaction reason"]
  Audit --> Check["Rights preservation check"]
  Exit --> Check
  Fork --> Check
  Dissent --> Check
  Redaction --> Check
  Check -->|preserved| Promote["Transition may proceed"]
  Check -->|missing or unsafe| Block["Block, redact with reason, or escalate"]
  Promote --> Evidence["Evidence record"]
  Block --> Evidence
```

**How to read the rights preservation check:** The preservation check treats audit, exit, fork, dissent, and redaction as runtime obligations. A governance transition may proceed only when those rights survive the transition; missing or unsafe rights block, redact with reason, or escalate.

## Interfaces

Governance rights become operational through a Governance Right Record. It is the point where political ideals become inspectable runtime obligations: who holds the right, what scope it covers, what artifacts are required, what access path exists, what safety constraints apply, what redaction reason is allowed, and how the right survives replacement.

- Governance issues rights.
- SCFs preserve rights across replacement.
- Execution logs evidence for audit.

Minimum fields:

- `right_id`
- `right_type`
- `request_state`
- `holder`
- `scope`
- `required_artifacts`
- `material_available`
- `material_withheld`
- `safety_constraints`
- `access_path`
- `denial_or_redaction_reason`
- `appeal_path`
- `expiry_or_revisit`
- `challenged_party_independence`
- `preservation_rule`
- `preservation_obligation`
- `receipt_refs`
- `test_hook`
- `non_claims`

Governance issues rights. SCFs preserve rights across replacement. Execution and memory systems produce audit artifacts. Evidence checks whether the right was materially usable rather than merely declared.

Material usability is the interface standard. A declared audit right is weak if records are missing, unreadable, unauthenticated, or controlled only by the challenged party. A declared exit path is weak if exports omit the state needed to continue elsewhere. A declared fork right is weak if it has no reproducible boundary or if it silently discards safety obligations. The record does not solve institutional governance by itself, but it gives later validators something concrete to inspect.

The rights-exercise record carries request state, material available, material withheld, appeal path, expiry or revisit condition, challenged-party independence, preservation obligation, receipt references, and non-claims. Those fields keep a rights record from collapsing into policy text. They ask whether the holder can actually obtain usable material, see what was withheld, challenge the decision somewhere other than the authority being challenged, and preserve the receipt across later transitions.

Rights outcomes need separate states: access failure, justified denial, redacted access, partial export, portable export, contested decision, and preserved dissent. Treating all non-success states as a generic refusal makes rights impossible to audit.

## Invariants

- Audit records cannot be silently deleted.
- Exit paths remain materially usable.
- Fork rights do not bypass safety constraints.

The invariant is practical availability. A right that cannot be exercised under realistic conditions is not preserved. A right that bypasses safety constraints is not a right; it is an authority leak.

Challenged-party independence protects review. The challenged authority may be part of the review path, but it cannot be the only place where the record, denial reason, or appeal path exists.

The rights layer must also preserve continuity across upgrades. A user should not lose audit, appeal, export, or fork paths because a model, memory system, policy engine, or steward agent was replaced. Rights attach to the field and the governed relationship, not to one convenient implementation. If a replacement cannot carry the rights receipt forward, the replacement is not ready for default use.

## Failure modes

- Governance capture.
- Data hostage-taking.
- Opaque automated policy updates.

Governance capture occurs when the same authority controls policy, logs, appeal, and revision. Data hostage-taking converts dependency into control. Opaque policy updates turn governance into a moving target. Each failure should produce a rights record violation, not merely a complaint.

Rights theater is the governance failure that hides behind agreeable language. The system publishes rights language, but the actual logs, exports, redaction rules, appeal channels, and preservation hooks are unavailable when the right would constrain the system.

Asymmetric exit makes the same problem concrete. A user may technically leave, but the system keeps the only usable memory, provenance, source links, or artifact graph. That turns exit into abandonment rather than autonomy. The governed path should preserve enough portable state for a person or project to continue elsewhere without granting unsafe access or erasing legitimate audit obligations.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The first rights surface is a `governance_right_record` schema plus fixtures for audit, exit, and fork. The first fixture should establish that an audit right has required artifacts, available and withheld material fields, an access path, appeal path, challenged-party independence note, preservation obligation, receipt references, and non-claims.

A rights receipt suite should make the governance surface tangible: one complete audit response, one justified redaction with appeal path, one exit export with portable state, and one fork request denied because safety obligations cannot transfer. The suite would test material usability while keeping institutional governance unclaimed.

The minimum should make withheld material visible as a recorded limitation, not an invisible absence.

It should also show that appeal and export routes have their own receipts rather than borrowing the audit receipt.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Governance rights have to become contestability infrastructure for the stack. It makes disagreement operational through portable artifacts, inspectable records, exit paths, audit rights, forkable state, appeal surfaces, and review receipts rather than treating governance as an external social layer.

Fork, exit, audit, dissent, appeal, and contestability become technical interfaces when they carry artifacts, receipts, portability guarantees, and safety limits. The contestability layer makes those rights usable through:

- Operating mechanism: Represent rights as capabilities and required interfaces; Attach audit artifacts to governance decisions; Treat fork/exit as preserved escape hatches against lock-in.
- Stack interface: Governance issues rights; SCFs preserve rights across replacement; Execution logs evidence for audit.
- Preserved invariants: Audit records cannot be silently deleted; Exit paths remain materially usable; Fork rights do not bypass safety constraints.
- Rights closure: governance capture, data hostage-taking, and opaque automated policy updates should create appeal records, fork or exit support, rollback, escalation, or residuals instead of disappearing into administrative state.

This contestability infrastructure remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. The rights claim belongs at `argument` until fork/export fixtures, audit-retention checks, appeal and exit-path tests, data-portability reviews, and safety-limited fork scenarios show that rights remain usable without becoming an authority bypass.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Governance right record validation | Check that the right fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Audit-record availability test | Check that required audit artifacts exist and can be accessed or redacted with a reason. | planned; not run |
| Exit-path preservation test | Check that export, revocation, and continuity paths remain materially usable. | planned; not run |
| Fork-right safety test | Check that forking preserves source, privacy, and safety obligations instead of bypassing them. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:governance.rights.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.GovernanceRights` | A governance transition preserves required audit and exit capabilities. | implemented |
| `lean:governance.rights.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.GovernanceRights` | A transition that removes a protected right is rejected or marked invalid. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over governance transition and protected-right removal reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: required audit and exit capabilities are preserved by a valid accepted transition, and removal of a protected right is rejected or marked invalid. They do not prove real access-path availability, fork safety, institutional contestability, or SCF replacement behavior.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ethica_mechanica` | Ethica Mechanica | alignment_constitution | Geometric-order philosophical/constitutional source. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for fork, exit, audit, dissent, and hard-coded rights |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector-only/source-note mapped |
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for functional rights, feedback, consent limits, and resistance |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | security_governance | Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for credential boundaries, handles, permission lookup, and SCIF isolation |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for governed axioms, auditable revision, sandboxing, versioning, and rollback |
| `uat` | Unified Adaptive Tribunal | evaluation_refinement | Multi-AI collaborative refinement/evaluation protocol. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for dossier-bounded verification, adversarial review, unsupported-claim removal, and SME sign-off |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available; tracked local-project passage reviewed for consent, non-domination, reversibility, auditability, memory rights, and self-modification review |

The crosswalk keeps governance rights tied to their source families while preserving the distinction between rights vocabulary, schema validation, reviewed source passages, connector-only synthesis, and actual institutional implementation.

## Summary

Governance Rights: Fork, Exit, and Audit turns contestability into interfaces. The point is not to decorate governance with ideals; it is to make audit, appeal, exit, fork, and dissent survive contact with runtime systems, memory, security, and capability replacement.

Those rights need a stable capability boundary. If a capability can be replaced, the user's rights must not depend on the old implementation staying in place. Rights receipts need a capability identity that survives upgrades.

A right should survive the transition that makes it inconvenient. If a model update, policy update, memory migration, or capability replacement removes the path to exercise the right, the governance layer has failed even if the policy text still sounds correct. This is why rights belong in the same architecture as field identity, rollback, audit logs, and release governance. They are not outside constraints imposed after the system improves. They are part of what improvement must preserve. The stronger the system becomes, the more important it is that contestability has addresses, receipts, owners, and failure modes rather than being left as a social promise.

## Handoff

Rights need stable targets. Stable Capability Fields follows because fork, exit, audit, rollback, and appeal cannot depend on a temporary implementation name that disappears during replacement. Stable fields give capabilities persistent identities, evaluator policies, evidence histories, and authority ceilings so governance rights can attach to what a capability is supposed to do, not merely to the current code path.
